title: 9 Best Herbal Teas For Oral Thrush
intro: Herbal teas for Oral thrush are a popular natural remedy used to treat the fungal infection caused by Candida, characterized by white patches on the tongue, mouth, and gums. These teas are effective in treating Oral thrush due to their antifungal, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to reduce the growth of Candida and promote a healthy oral environment. Some examples of herbal teas used to treat Oral thrush include Peppermint tea, which helps to reduce inflammation and kill bacteria, Chamomile tea, which soothes and calms the mouth, and Echinacea tea, which boosts the immune system to fight off the infection. Additionally, other herbal teas such as Ginger tea, Sage tea, and Licorice root tea are also used to treat Oral thrush due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties, which help to prevent the growth of Candida and promote healing.

preparations.herb_desc: Echinacea purpurea teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection that affects the mucous membranes of the mouth. The anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties of this herbal preparation help to soothe the affected areas and combat the fungal growth, thereby reducing the severity of the symptoms. The bioactive constituents, including alkylamides, caffeic acid, and chicoric acid, exhibit antifungal and immunomodulatory activities that aid in the recovery from oral thrush. Regular consumption of Echinacea purpurea teas has been found to promote healing, reduce inflammation, and prevent the recurrence of the infection, making it a beneficial natural remedy for this condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Echinacea purpurea flowers and leaves.
Combine the flowers and leaves with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, then strain it into another cup using a tea strainer or cheesecloth.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea, if desired, to help soothe the throat.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help alleviate symptoms of oral thrush.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Echinacea purpurea teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, digestive issues, and interactions with other medications due to its immunomodulatory properties. Precautions should be taken when using Echinacea purpurea teas, such as avoiding excessive consumption, monitoring blood sugar levels, and being aware of potential interactions with other herbs or supplements, especially for individuals with pre-existing conditions or taking medications.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Echinacea purpurea tea 3 times a day
Use 1 tablespoon of dried Echinacea roots per cup of boiling water
Steep for 5-7 minutes and strain before drinking
Continue using tea for 7-10 days to see improvement
Consult a healthcare professional if symptoms persist or worsen

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, also known as ginger tea, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, an infection caused by the Candida fungus. The anti-inflammatory and antifungal properties of ginger tea help to reduce inflammation and kill the fungus, thereby treating the infection. The bioactive constituents of ginger tea, including gingerols and shogaols, exhibit antimicrobial activity, which contributes to its effectiveness in treating oral thrush. Drinking ginger tea may help to alleviate symptoms of oral thrush, such as soreness and difficulty swallowing, and may also help to prevent the recurrence of the infection.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Zingiber officinale (Ginger) roots and 1 cup of water.
Peel and chop the ginger roots into small pieces.
Boil the chopped ginger in 1 cup of water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture and discard the ginger pieces.
Let the tea cool and use it as a mouthwash 2-3 times a day to help soothe oral thrush.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Journal of ethnopharmacology", Zingiber officinale teas for oral thrush have not been directly investigated for this specific condition, however, the study found that the methanolic extract of Z. officinale enhanced the antimicrobial potency of berberine, which could potentially be applied to treat oral thrush by inhibiting efflux pumps in bacteria.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea due to its active compounds interacting with the digestive system. To use Zingiber officinale teas safely, take precautions such as starting with small doses, avoiding consumption on an empty stomach, and being cautious with individuals who have sensitive stomachs or are prone to digestive issues.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Zingiber officinale tea 3 times a day.
Use 1 teaspoon of dried Zingiber officinale for each cup of tea.
Steep the tea for 5-7 minutes to allow for proper infusion.
Consume the tea for a period of 7-14 days to manage symptoms.
Consult a healthcare professional for further guidance and advice.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection caused by Candida species, due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents present in these teas, including glycyrrhizin, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, help to inhibit the growth of Candida albicans and reduce inflammation in the oral cavity. By reducing the fungal load and alleviating inflammation, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can help to alleviate symptoms of oral thrush, such as soreness, redness, and white patches in the mouth. The benefits of using Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to treat oral thrush include their safety, efficacy, and ability to promote oral health without the need for antibiotics, making them a valuable natural remedy for this condition.
preparation_procedure: Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the root.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Indian journal of dental research : official publication of Indian Society for Dental Research", Glycyrrhiza glabra teas showed antifungal activity against oral Candida albicans, with a mean zone of inhibition measuring 19.8 ± 0.83 at 24 hours.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but they may cause side effects such as an increase in blood pressure and electrolyte imbalances due to the high levels of glycyrrhizin, a compound that can mimic the effects of aldosterone. To use Glycyrrhiza glabra teas safely, it is essential to consume them in moderation and avoid taking them for extended periods, as prolonged use can lead to adverse effects on the heart, kidneys, and other bodily systems.
preparation_usage: Rinse your mouth with warm water before using Glycyrrhiza glabra tea.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra roots in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Drink 1 cup of the tea 3 times a day, after meals and before bedtime.
Soak a cotton swab in the tea and apply it to the affected area for 5 minutes.
Repeat the process for 7-10 days to see noticeable improvements in oral thrush symptoms.

preparations.herb_desc: Eucalyptus globulus teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection caused by Candida albicans, due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents present in Eucalyptus globulus teas, such as cineole, limonene, and beta-pinene, exhibit potent antifungal activity, helping to combat the infection and alleviate symptoms. Drinking Eucalyptus globulus teas may help to soothe the mucous membranes, reduce inflammation, and promote healing in the affected areas, thereby providing relief from oral thrush. The benefits of using Eucalyptus globulus teas to treat oral thrush include its natural and non-invasive approach, reduced risk of side effects, and potential prevention of the development of antibiotic-resistant strains of Candida.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Eucalyptus globulus leaves, 1 cup of boiling water, and a strainer.
Steep the Eucalyptus globulus leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup, discard the leaves.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey (optional) to the tea and stir well.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Eucalyptus globulus teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as an allergic reaction, digestive issues, and skin irritation in some individuals. To minimize risks, it is essential to use this herbal preparation in moderation, avoid consuming it with other medications, and monitor your body's response closely, especially if you have sensitive skin or allergies.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Eucalyptus globulus tea, 3 times a day.
Hold the tea in your mouth for 5 minutes after drinking.
Swish the tea around your mouth, then spit it out.
Repeat the process 3-4 times a day for 2 weeks.
Use 1 tablespoon of dried Eucalyptus globulus leaves per cup of boiling water.

preparations.herb_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, an oral fungal infection, due to their antifungal properties. The antiseptic properties of this herbal preparation help to prevent the growth of Candida albicans, the fungus responsible for oral thrush, while its antimicrobial properties combat other microorganisms that may exacerbate the condition. The bioactive constituents present in Lavandula angustifolia teas, such as linalool and linalyl acetate, contribute to their antifungal and antimicrobial activities, helping to alleviate the symptoms of oral thrush. By using Lavandula angustifolia teas, individuals can benefit from a natural and non-invasive treatment option that promotes oral health and reduces the risk of complications associated with oral thrush.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Lavandula angustifolia flowers.
Combine the dried flowers with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Cover the cup and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a separate cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Let the tea cool before drinking 1/2 cup, 3-4 times a day.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Lavandula angustifolia teas can be used as a natural remedy to treat oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, digestive issues, and interactions with other medications. Precautions include using the tea in moderation, avoiding it if you have liver or kidney problems, and being aware of potential interactions with other herbs, as excessive consumption may worsen the condition or cause additional health concerns.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Lavandula angustifolia tea 3 times a day.
Swish 1 tablespoon of tea in your mouth for 2 minutes.
Spit out the tea and do not swallow it.
Repeat steps 2 and 3 3 times a day.
Continue for 7-10 days to help clear oral thrush.

preparations.herb_desc: Cymbopogon citratus teas, also known as lemongrass tea, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection that affects the mouth. The anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties of this herbal preparation help to treat oral thrush by reducing inflammation, killing bacteria, and inhibiting the growth of fungi. The bioactive constituents of lemongrass tea, including citral, geraniol, and limonene, possess antifungal and antibacterial activities that help to combat oral thrush. The benefits of using lemongrass tea to treat oral thrush include its ability to reduce symptoms such as mouth pain, white patches, and bad breath, promoting a healthy oral environment.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Cymbopogon citratus leaves and 2 cups of water.
Chop the leaves into small pieces and place them in a large pot.
Add the chopped leaves and 2 cups of water to the pot and bring to a boil.
Reduce the heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea.
Drink 1/4 cup of the tea 3-4 times a day to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Antibiotics (Basel, Switzerland)", Cymbopogon citratus teas have shown potential in fighting oral biofilm, specifically reducing it to low concentrations.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cymbopogon citratus teas can be used to treat oral thrush due to its antimicrobial and antifungal properties, but it may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with medications like blood thinners and diabetes medications. Precautions when using Cymbopogon citratus teas to treat oral thrush include avoiding excessive consumption, as it may lead to digestive issues, and monitoring blood sugar levels if you have diabetes, as the tea may affect blood sugar control.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Cymbopogon citratus tea twice daily
Hold the tea in your mouth for 1-2 minutes before swallowing
Repeat the process after 2 hours if thrush symptoms persist
Continue drinking tea for 5-7 days for optimal results
Monitor your symptoms and adjust dosage as directed by a doctor

preparations.herb_desc: Cinchona officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection caused by Candida species. preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cinchona officinalis teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but they may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications, including blood thinners. To use Cinchona officinalis teas safely, it's essential to avoid taking them if you have kidney or liver problems, and to be cautious when combining them with other medications or supplements, especially those that affect blood clotting or blood sugar levels.

Drink 2 cups of Cinchona officinalis tea, 3 times a day, for 2-3 weeks.

preparations.herb_desc: Melaleuca alternifolia teas, also known as tea tree oil, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush due to their antifungal and antibacterial properties. These properties help to reduce the growth of Candida albicans, the fungus that causes oral thrush, and soothe the inflamed mucous membranes in the mouth. The bioactive constituents of tea tree oil, including cineole and terpinen-4-ol, have been shown to exhibit potent antifungal activity, making it an effective treatment for this ailment. The benefits of using Melaleuca alternifolia teas to treat oral thrush include reduced symptoms, improved oral health, and a decrease in the risk of complications associated with this condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Melaleuca alternifolia leaves.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Steep the leaves in the boiling water for 5 minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard the leaves.
Drink 1 cup of the tea, 3 times a day for oral thrush relief.

preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melaleuca alternifolia teas can potentially cause gastrointestinal side effects, such as stomach upset, nausea, and diarrhea, when used to treat oral thrush due to its antimicrobial properties. To minimize potential interactions, individuals should be cautious when consuming other medications or supplements, especially those affecting blood sugar levels or blood thinning, while using Melaleuca alternifolia teas for oral thrush treatment.
preparation_usage: Dissolve 1 teaspoon of Melaleuca alternifolia powder in 1 cup of warm water.
Swish the solution in your mouth for 1-2 minutes, 3 times a day.
Spit out the solution and do not swallow it.
Repeat the process for 3-5 days or as directed by a healthcare professional.

preparations.herb_desc: Thymus vulgaris teas, also known as thyme tea, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush due to its antifungal and antibacterial properties. The bioactive constituents, such as thymol and carvacrol, in this herbal preparation help to inhibit the growth of Candida albicans, the fungus responsible for oral thrush. Thymus vulgaris teas can help to treat oral thrush by reducing inflammation, relieving symptoms, and preventing the overgrowth of the fungus. The benefits of this herbal preparation include its non-invasive nature, minimal side effects, and ability to promote a healthy oral environment, making it a popular alternative to conventional treatments.

Steep the thyme leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup to remove the thyme leaves.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the thyme tea to make it sweeter.
Drink the thyme tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Complementary therapies in medicine, Thymus vulgaris teas, also known as thyme, used in sage tea-thyme-peppermint hydrosol oral rinse, showed promising results in alleviating oral mucositis, which may also suggest its potential benefits in treating oral thrush.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus vulgaris teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but possible side effects may include allergic reactions, digestive issues, and interactions with other medications, particularly blood thinners and diabetes medications. preparations.herb_desc: Mentha x piperita teas, also known as peppermint teas, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents, including menthol, menthone, and limonene, in these teas help to inhibit the growth of Candida albicans, the fungus responsible for oral thrush. By reducing the fungal load and soothing the oral mucosa, Mentha x piperita teas can help to alleviate the symptoms of oral thrush, such as pain, swelling, and difficulty swallowing. The benefits of using Mentha x piperita teas to treat oral thrush include their ability to provide quick relief, promote healing, and prevent the recurrence of the infection.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water and 1/4 cup of fresh Mentha x piperita leaves.
Steep the leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture using a tea strainer or cheesecloth to remove the leaves.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea mixture, if desired, to make it sweeter.
Drink the tea mixture 2-3 times a day to help manage oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Mycoses, Mentha x piperita teas for oral thrush may be beneficial due to the high anticandidal activity of M. piperita, which had an inhibition zone of 12.3 mm and a minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) value of 1.25 mg ml(-1) against Candida albicans.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Mentha x piperita teas can be used to help alleviate the symptoms of oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as digestive issues, allergic reactions, and interaction with other medications due to its menthol and menthone content. To use Mentha x piperita teas safely, take precautions by starting with small doses, avoiding consumption if you have sensitive teeth or gums, and monitoring your body's response to the herbal preparation to minimize potential side effects.

preparations.herb_desc: Taraxacum officinale teas, also known as dandelion tea, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection caused by Candida. The anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties of this herbal preparation help to soothe and heal the affected areas, reducing discomfort and pain associated with the ailment. The bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpene lactones, in Taraxacum officinale teas exhibit antifungal activity, inhibiting the growth of Candida and promoting a healthy oral environment. The benefits of using Taraxacum officinale teas to treat oral thrush include reduced symptoms, accelerated recovery, and a lower risk of complications, making it a valuable natural remedy for this common condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers, or 2 teaspoons of dried leaves and flowers.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Add the Taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes. Strain the mixture and discard the solids.
Drink 1/2 cup of the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of ethnopharmacology, Taraxacum officinale teas for oral thrush is not specifically mentioned in the provided study, but the study highlights that Clematis brachiata (leaves) displayed noteworthy activity against all three Candida spp., which includes Candida albicans, a common cause of oral thrush.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Taraxacum officinale teas can be used as a natural remedy to treat oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as allergic reactions, stomach upset, and diarrhea in some individuals. Precautions should be taken when using Taraxacum officinale teas, including avoiding its use in pregnant or breastfeeding women, as well as those with sensitive stomachs or digestive issues.

preparations.herb_desc: Cinnamomum verum teas have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, an infection caused by the fungus Candida albicans. The antifungal and antibacterial properties of this herbal preparation help to inhibit the growth of fungal cells and promote a healthy oral environment. The bioactive constituents, including cinnamaldehyde and linalool, exhibit potent antifungal activity, thereby aiding in the treatment of oral thrush. By consuming Cinnamomum verum teas, individuals can reap the benefits of a natural remedy that not only alleviates symptoms but also promotes long-term oral health and reduces the risk of recurrence.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water, 1 tablespoon of Cinnamomum verum bark, and a strainer.
Boil the water and add the Cinnamomum verum bark.
Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the mixture.
Drink 1/2 cup of the tea, 2-3 times a day, to help soothe oral thrush symptoms.
Repeat the process for 7-10 days, or as directed by a healthcare professional.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Advances in experimental medicine and biology, Cinnamomum verum teas for oral thrush may have beneficial effects as they are identified as one of the herbal medicines useful in the treatment of C. albicans in oral candidiasis.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cinnamomum verum teas can be used as a potential treatment for oral thrush, but it may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, including nausea and diarrhea, due to its high cinnamon oil content. To minimize potential side effects, it's essential to use Cinnamomum verum teas in moderation and monitor your body's response, avoiding excessive consumption and mixing with other medications, especially blood thinners.

preparations.herb_desc: Thymus serpyllum teas, also known as wild thyme tea, have been traditionally used to treat oral thrush, a fungal infection that affects the mouth. The antifungal and antibacterial properties of this herbal preparation help to treat oral thrush by reducing the growth of Candida albicans, the fungus responsible for the infection. The bioactive constituents of Thymus serpyllum, including thymol, carvacrol, and linalool, contribute to its antifungal activity and ability to combat oral thrush. Drinking Thymus serpyllum teas may provide relief from symptoms of oral thrush, such as oral pain, redness, and white patches, and may also help to prevent the recurrence of the infection.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Thymus serpyllum leaves and flowers.
Measure 1 tablespoon of dried Thymus serpyllum leaves and flowers, or 2 cups of fresh ones, and place them in a heat-resistant cup or mug.
Pour 8 oz (1 cup) of boiling water over the Thymus serpyllum leaves and flowers.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid to remove the solids.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe and prevent oral thrush.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus serpyllum teas can be used to treat oral thrush, but possible side effects include gastrointestinal upset, such as nausea or diarrhea, and allergic reactions like skin rashes or itching. Precautions to take when using this herbal preparation include starting with small amounts to assess tolerance, avoiding it if you have sensitive stomach or allergies, and being cautious with interactions with other medications, especially blood thinners.

preparation_study: According to PeerJ, Echinacea purpurea teas for oral thrush may have some potential benefit as the extracts of Echinacea purpurea flowers exhibited an anti-inflammatory effect in human tonsil epithelial cells, which could potentially be used in the management of painful inflammation due to streptococcal pharyngitis, however, there is no direct evidence in the provided study for its use in oral thrush.
intro_study: According to "Nihon Ishinkin Gakkai zasshi = Japanese journal of medical mycology", teas for oral thrush may be beneficial in the prevention or treatment of oral candidiasis due to the protective actions of certain herbal preparations such as tea tree oil and clove preparation.
